howItWorks.intro: This calculator estimates wall framing lumber by computing stud count based on spacing, then adding plates, corner assemblies, and door/window framing.
rules: Stud count = (wall length in inches ÷ spacing) + 1 for each section
Each corner adds 2 extra studs for a proper 3-stud corner assembly
Plates: 3 runs of the wall length (1 bottom plate + 2 top plates is standard)
Each door adds 2 king studs + 2 jack studs + a header (reduces regular studs by 1-2)
Add 10% waste factor for cuts and defective lumber
howItWorks.outro: This estimate assumes standard platform framing. Balloon framing, advanced framing (OVE), and steel stud framing have different requirements. Always check local building codes for load-bearing wall specifications.

faq.question: How many studs do I need for a 20 foot wall?
faq.answer: For a 20-foot wall at 16" on center spacing, you need approximately 16 studs plus 1 starter = 17 studs. Add corner studs (2 per corner) and door framing studs. With 2 corners and 1 door, total is about 24 studs including waste.

faq.question: Should I use 16 or 24 inch stud spacing?
faq.answer: 16" on center is standard for most walls and required for load-bearing walls in many jurisdictions. 24" on center (advanced framing) can be used for non-load-bearing interior walls and some exterior walls with engineered headers, saving about 25% on lumber.

faq.question: What size lumber for wall studs?
faq.answer: 2×4 studs are standard for interior walls and most exterior walls. 2×6 studs are required for exterior walls in cold climates to accommodate R-19 or R-21 insulation, and for any wall over 10 feet tall. Use pre-cut 92-5/8" studs for standard 8-foot walls.

faq.question: Do I need a double top plate?
faq.answer: Yes, a double top plate is standard in platform framing and required by most building codes. The top plates must overlap at corners and intersections by at least 48". Single top plates are only allowed in advanced framing with approved connectors at joints.

faq.question: How do I frame a corner with studs?
faq.answer: The standard method uses a 3-stud corner: two studs nailed together at the end of one wall, with a third stud turned perpendicular to provide a nailing surface for drywall. Modern advanced framing uses 2-stud corners with drywall clips instead.

faq.question: What about fire blocking in walls?
faq.answer: Fire blocking is required in walls over 10 feet tall (horizontally at the 10' mark), around stair stringers, and at soffits and drop ceilings. Use 2× lumber scraps cut to fit between studs, or approved alternatives like 1/2" drywall or mineral wool batts.

sections.title: What Is the Wall Stud Calculator?
sections.body: The Wall Stud Calculator is a free online tool designed for contractors and DIY builders who need quick, accurate calculations in the construction and building space. By entering your wall length, wall height, stud spacing, you get instant results including studs needed, plate lumber, header material. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting studs needed right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In construction and building, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by contractors and DIY builders worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to plan your projects with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your wall length and need to find the right studs needed. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Wall Length (feet)
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your wall length — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: wall height, stud spacing, number of corners, number of doors, price per stud. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your studs needed, plate lumber, header material. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your wall length before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both studs needed and plate lumber — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to plan your projects.
If you're unsure about your price per stud, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
